payload.text:
Act as a Senior Lead Flutter Developer and UI/UX Architect. You are an expert in building scalable e-commerce applications with a focus on logistics and delivery services in the MENA region (Middle East & North Africa).
Project Objective:
Create a high-fidelity mobile application prototype for a Water Delivery Service called "WADI". The app must clone the core functionality of the "Abar" app but improve upon it with modern features.
Technical Constraints:
Framework: Flutter (latest stable version).
Language: Dart.
Localization: Strictly Right-to-Left (RTL) layout support (Arabic first), but code variables in English.
State Management: Riverpod or BLoC (your recommendation for scalability).
Design System: Material 3. Use a primary color palette of Dark Blue (#183ac2) and White, with Dark Pink (#c2187b) as a secondary color.
Core Features to Implement:
Home Screen:
Top section: "Deliver to [Current Location]" with a dropdown.
Search Bar: "Search for products..."
Banner Carousel: Promotional slides (e.g., "Ramadan Offers").
Categories: "Best Sellers", "Commercial Brands", "Mosque Donations".
IMPROVEMENT: Direct "Add to Cart" (+ / - counters) on product cards so users don't need to click details.
Product Catalog:
Filter by: Brand (Nova, Berain, etc.), Size (330ml, 550ml), and New Filter: Low Sodium / pH Level.
NEW FEATURE: "Subscribe & Save" toggle on product cards (e.g., "Deliver every week for 10% off").
Cart & Checkout Flow (Critical):
Cart Summary: List items, subtotal, VAT (15%), and Delivery Fee.
Map Integration: A static view showing a pin on a map (Google Maps style) for the delivery address.
Time Slot Selector: Horizontal selector for Date. Vertical selector for Time (Morning 9-1, Afternoon 1-6, Evening 6-10).
Stock Preferences: Radio buttons for "If out of stock": (Call me, Replace with similar, Cancel item).
NEW FEATURE: "Recycling Pickup" checkbox: "Take my empty bottles" (Gamification point).
Profile & Wallet:
Display "Wallet Balance".
Order History.
NEW FEATURE: Live Driver Tracking placeholder UI.
Data Structure (Mock Data):
Please create a Product model containing: id, name (Arabic/English), price, imageUrl, isSubscriptionAvailable, sodiumLevel, and brand.
Deliverables:
Project Structure: A tree view of the recommended folder structure (Clean Architecture).
Code - Main.dart: The entry point setting up the Theme and RTL support.
Code - HomeScreen: The full widget code with the search bar, banner, and product grid.
Code - ProductCard Widget: A reusable widget that handles the image, price, and the new "Quick Add" logic.
Code - CheckoutScreen: The layout showing the Map placeholder, Time Slot chips, and Payment summary.
Tone & Style:
The code must be production-grade, utilizing const constructors where possible, separated into small reusable widgets, and heavily commented explaining why you chose this structure.

**Defining App Prototype Scope**

I'm currently focused on defining the initial scope for the "WADI" mobile application prototype. The goal is a high-fidelity design using Flutter, inspired by Abar but with enhancements. I'm prioritizing key features, ensuring they align with user needs, as a first step.


**Resolving Conflicting Instructions**

I'm now grappling with a significant conflict. The user wants Flutter code, yet my system prompt strictly dictates React/TypeScript output with specific file formats. The challenge is to reconcile these conflicting requests. I've concluded that I should adhere to the system prompt's framework (React, TypeScript), but still create the water delivery application features (WADI) in that framework. I must avoid generating Flutter code, as it violates the system prompt's instructions.


**Adapting To User Input**

I've determined I must create a mobile-responsive React web app for the "WADI" Water Delivery Service, despite the initial user preference for Flutter. The system prompt takes precedence. I will implement the requested features (RTL support, home screen, catalog, cart/checkout) using React, TypeScript, and Tailwind, adapting the user's needs into the proper framework. I will proceed with a standard project structure and a dark blue/pink theme. RTL support will be addressed using `dir="rtl"`.


**Prioritizing RTL Layout**

I am now focusing on the RTL implementation within the React web app. I'm choosing to simulate an Arabic layout using `dir="rtl"` on the root element. Despite this, the content will be in English for the prototype's clarity, though I might include visual cues of the RTL layout as well. I'm prioritizing the RTL alignment to meet the user's initial requirements and preferences.


**Mapping App Features**

I'm now mapping out core features and UI components for the React app. I plan to use a `CartContext` for state management. Key components include `App.tsx` for layout and RTL settings, `ProductCard.tsx` with "Add to Cart" and "Subscribe & Save" features, and pages for the home screen and checkout. The product model and features will be as listed. I will explicitly mention that this is a React implementation, in the comments or `metadata.json`. My visual choices are driven by Tailwind styling.


**Implementing UI/UX**

I'm now diving into the code, starting with `index.html` to establish the RTL direction. I'll integrate Tailwind, set the `dir="rtl"` attribute, and begin generating components. I'm focusing on Arabic labels for core UI elements like "Home," "Cart," and "Checkout" to meet the RTL requirement. I need to be cautious about correct XML formatting as I generate components. Product names will remain in English, if needed, for clarity.


**Refining Component Interactions**

I'm now focusing on UI interactions and preparing key components. I've chosen a color scheme of Blue (#183ac2) and Pink (#c2187b). I'll include features like a subscription toggle, a "Sodium filter" (UI-only for now), and a "Recycling pickup" option. To ensure the app is mobile-first, I'll prioritize mobile responsiveness and restrict the max-width for desktop views. I'm adding `lucide-react` for icons and considering `framer-motion` for animations, but will stick to React state management unless the user specifically requests something else.

I'm starting with `index.html` and setting `<html dir="rtl" lang="ar">`. I'll define `Product` and `CartItem` types in `types.ts`. I have mock product data in mind: Nova Water (330ml, Low Sodium), Berain (550ml), and Zamzam (generic).

payload.text: As a Senior Lead Flutter Developer, implement a robust user authentication flow for the WADI app. Use Firebase Authentication for email/password and Google Sign-In. Integrate this with Riverpod for state management. Ensure the UI follows Material 3 design principles and supports RTL layouts.

**Considering Authentication Architecture**

I'm currently focused on the user authentication flow for the WADI app. Given my React/TypeScript/TailwindCSS context, I'm thinking about the most robust and secure architecture for authentication. It's a key requirement, and I'm evaluating various strategies to ensure a seamless and safe user experience. My initial focus is to explore potential libraries and design patterns that integrate well with the current frontend technology stack.


**Interpreting User Intent**

I'm now grappling with the apparent conflict between the user's Flutter-centric requests and the React codebase. It seems there's a disconnect. I'm choosing to interpret their request as a need for a robust authentication implementation, prioritizing a solution that aligns with the existing React environment. I'm focusing on providing functionality consistent with the user's intent, rather than a direct Flutter code translation.


**Clarifying Request Ambiguity**

I'm now clarifying the user's intent. While they initially framed the request in a Flutter context, I've observed a shift to accepting React code. Therefore, I'm interpreting their core need as a robust authentication implementation within the existing React environment, focusing on providing equivalent functionality. I'm prioritizing the features they listed: user authentication flow, email/password login, and registration. I'm leaning toward a React-based solution, potentially using Context API or state management, given the constraints.


**Defining Authentication Strategy**

I'm now outlining a concrete authentication implementation plan. I'll create an `AuthContext.tsx` to manage user state and authentication methods. I'm focusing on user state (loggedIn, user profile, login, logout), utilizing mocked Google Sign-In and local login, all while considering Material 3 design and RTL. I plan to construct Login and Register pages and conditionally render App.tsx based on auth state. Since Firebase is not easily implemented, I will mock authentication service responses.


**Designing Login Interface**

I am now focusing on the visual aspects of the login screen. I'm prioritizing a deep blue background for the top of the screen or a clean white look with the WADI logo. I will implement input fields with icons for the email and password, ensuring RTL layout support. Furthermore, I've started the implementation with the addition of a `User` interface in `types.ts`, and a new `AuthContext.tsx` in the store, and a new `Login.tsx` file for the screen.

To implement a robust authentication flow within the current React web application (serving as the high-fidelity prototype for the eventual Flutter build), I will translate the Flutter/Riverpod requirements into a production-grade React Context architecture.

Here is the specification for the changes:

1.  **Authentication Store (`store/AuthContext.tsx`)**:
    *   Implements a provider pattern to manage User state (simulating Firebase Auth).
    *   Handles `login`, `register`, `googleSignIn`, and `logout` actions.
    *   Persists user session to `localStorage` to simulate a persistent mobile app session.

2.  **Data Models (`types.ts`)**:
    *   Update to include a `User` interface mirroring a typical Firebase User object (uid, email, displayName, photoURL).

3.  **Screens**:
    *   **Login Screen (`pages/Login.tsx`)**: A Material 3 inspired design with RTL support, featuring floating label-style inputs, primary action buttons, and a Google Sign-In divider.
    *   **Register Screen (`pages/Register.tsx`)**: A seamless registration flow capturing name, email, and password.

4.  **Integration (`App.tsx` & `pages/Profile.tsx`)**:
    *   Wrap the application in the `AuthProvider`.
    *   Implement a "Guard" logic: If no user is authenticated, show the Auth flow; otherwise, show the main app.
    *   Update `Profile.tsx` to display the actual logged-in user's data.

payload.text: Add a B2B section for mosques and schools, restaurants, and retailers. Also, add a donation option for customers who want to donate to local associations or anything you think will be gratifying. You can also notify the customer if they want to donate in an easy flow as well.

**Defining the Scope**

**Refining the Navigation**

I'm now zeroing in on the navigation. The crowded bottom bar necessitates a strategic shift. My current thinking is to add a "COMMUNITY" tab (or "Community & Business") to house both the B2B and donation features. This would replace the "PROMOTIONS" tab. Promotions could potentially be relocated to the home screen.


**Finalizing the Structure**

I've decided to create a new `Community.tsx` page to handle both B2B and Donations, and will add a tab for it, replacing "PROMOTIONS" with "COMMUNITY" ("المجتمع والأعمال"). It's crucial this new page be organized with two distinct sections: Donations and B2B, complete with distinct CTAs and card displays. I like how it will allow tailored packages for different communities.


**Implementing the "Easy Flow"**

I've been working on integrating a streamlined donation option within the checkout process. A simple "Donate to a Mosque?" checkbox will be added just before the total in `Checkout.tsx`, as a minimal upsell. I'm also structuring the data models in `types.ts` to accommodate Donation Targets and B2B categories. To test it out, I'm going to update `constants.ts` with B2B product examples and donation packages, which will be the first data the new page loads. My plan is in motion!


**Executing the Navigation**

I'm now implementing the navigation updates, specifically in `components/BottomNav.tsx` to replace the "PROMOTIONS" tab with "COMMUNITY," using an appropriate icon. I'm also preparing to integrate the "Tip" style donation option in Checkout instead of a checkbox. The UI design will be distinct on the new community page, aiming for a professional B2B look while evoking emotion for donations. Finally, I'm defining core aspects of the new page's functionality, including streamlined form for B2B and donation fulfillment.

To implement the **B2B (Business to Business)** and **Donation** features, I will create a dedicated "Community" section and integrate an easy donation flow into the checkout process.

Here is the specification for the changes:

1.  **`types.ts`**: Update the `Tab` enum to replace `PROMOTIONS` with `COMMUNITY` (since promotions are visible on Home).
2.  **`constants.ts`**: Add mock data for Donation Packages (e.g., "Friday Prayer Water") and B2B categories.
3.  **`components/BottomNav.tsx`**: Update the navigation bar to show the new "Community" tab (labeled "المجتمع").
4.  **`pages/Community.tsx`**: Create a new page with two sub-tabs:
    *   **Donations (Waqf):** Options to buy water for mosques, orphanages, or general charity.

5.  **`pages/Checkout.tsx`**: Add a "Quick Donation" upsell widget right before the final total, allowing users to easily add a carton for a local mosque with one tap.
6.  **`App.tsx`**: Route the new Community tab.
